questions.question: Explain what a stochastic matrix is and state the key property that defines it. Provide a simple 2x2 example.
questions.modelAnswer: A stochastic matrix is a square matrix used to describe transitions in a Markov chain. Each entry represents a probability (non-negative). Each row must sum to 1, ensuring the probabilities of moving from a given state account for all possibilities. Example: [[0.8, 0.2], [0.6, 0.4]]

questions.question: Describe the significance of the steady-state vector (or equilibrium distribution) of a stochastic matrix. Explain how one can find it, referencing the relevant linear algebra concept.
questions.modelAnswer: The steady-state vector represents the long-term probabilities of being in each state of the system. It is found by solving the equation πP = π, where P is the stochastic matrix and π is the probability vector. This is equivalent to finding the eigenvector corresponding to the eigenvalue λ=1.

questions.question: A website user's navigation can be modeled as a Markov chain with three pages: Home (H), Products (P), and Contact (C). The stochastic matrix P is given. Calculate the probability that a user who starts on the Home page is on the Contact page after two steps. Then, discuss whether this Markov chain is regular and what the long-term behavior implies for the website designer.
questions.modelAnswer: The probability is found by computing the (H, C) entry of the matrix P². A chain is regular if some power of P has all positive entries, meaning every state can be reached from every other state in a finite number of steps. The long-term behavior, given by the steady-state vector, shows the proportion of time a random user spends on each page, which is crucial for layout and advertising decisions.
